evidence_records.raw_text:
| Time step | O&M sample text |
|---|---|
| T1 | Define the leaking branch on Helion PackLine-327 EV as the battery coolant quick connector at either the inlet or outlet side, and note whether coolant loss is active, dried, pressure-dependent, or only found after the vehicle sits overnight. Keep the opening complaint tied to the exact connector position and leak behavior so later pressure checks stay on the same repaired branch. |
| T2 | Isolate the pack, drain only the affected local cooling branch, and remove the side shield so the quick connector, retaining clip, male tube, O-ring land, hose end, and nearby support bracket are fully exposed for replacement. Keep the connector, male tube, and bracket geometry visible together because hose side-load often returns only when the support hardware is restored. |
| T3 | Release the retaining clip in a controlled way, disconnect the quick connector, and inspect the latch windows, inner O-ring seat, male tube surface, insertion stop, and hose-end condition for scoring, flattening, cracked plastic, or remnants of the old seal. This first read needs to distinguish seal damage from tube scoring before new parts are fitted and the original witness pattern is lost. |
| T4 | Clean the male tube and confirm that no old O-ring fragment, dried coolant crystal, burr, or contamination remains on the sealing land, because even a small scratch or trapped debris can cut the new seal during reassembly. The sealing land has to remain clean and continuous from insertion stop to O-ring track before the replacement connector is pushed home. |
| T5 | Install the replacement quick connector with the correct O-ring orientation, confirm full tube insertion against the stop, and verify that the retaining clip sits flush and symmetric in both lock windows instead of riding one side of the connector body. |
| T6 | Restore the hose support and bracket position so the new connector is not held in side load, checking hose twist, clamp clocking, bracket alignment, and the natural free angle of the hose once the support hardware is returned. The connector should leave the bracket and hose span in a neutral installed state, not in a forced alignment that will reopen the leak after circulation begins. |
| T7 | Refill and bleed the affected cooling branch, watching the reservoir response, bleed return, and the new quick connector for trapped air release, fluid pulsing, or fresh wetting during the first circulation cycle. Record whether the first visible wetting belongs to trapped-air purge or to a true seal failure at the new connector body or O-ring seat. |
| T8 | Hold pressure on the repaired branch and confirm that the new connector body, O-ring seat, retaining clip, hose orientation, and bracket support remain dry under both static hold and pump-induced flow. This pressure hold has to prove both sealing and neutral support geometry after the branch settles back into its normal loaded route. |
| T9 | Restore all access parts and close the job only after the replacement record includes the exact connector location, removed failure mode, new connector seating confirmation, bleed result, and final dry-check outcome. The closeout should identify the exact replaced connector and the final dry result in the same repaired branch so the next service event starts from one named hardware boundary. |

Keep the opening complaint tied to the exact connector position and leak behavior so later pressure checks stay on the same repaired branch.

evidence_records.raw_text:
| Time step | O&M sample text |
|---|---|
| T1 | Start the enclosure-seal replacement on Arcline Logic-579 LR by reviewing the moisture complaint or prior leak history and checking the BMS enclosure perimeter first, so the new seal is not installed over a lid, flange, or pass-through defect that would immediately recreate the same leak path. Record which side or corner showed water tracks, because an upper left pass-through leak and a lower flange seep have different boundaries even though both wet the same enclosure. |
| T2 | Depower the controller, remove the BMS lid, and extract the old seal while tracking the original joint position, flattened sections, adhesive residue, and any corner where the gasket appears pinched, under-compressed, or damaged by previous service work. Keep the removed seal indexed so the final failure can still be tied to one joint, one bolt-boss transition, or one connector pass-through corner after the groove has been cleaned. |
| T3 | Clean the full seal groove, bolt-boss transitions, connector pass-through corners, and lid flange, then inspect for scratches, flange distortion, trapped dirt, previous seal scrap, or burrs; dry-fit the lid if needed to confirm flatness, dowel alignment, and even flange contact before the new gasket goes in. This preparation step should separate a bad gasket from a damaged sealing surface, because a rolled flange or raised burr will defeat any replacement seal installed over it. |
| T4 | Install the replacement enclosure seal with the joint placed away from the known water path, confirming that the gasket sits fully in the groove along straight runs and remains untwisted around the corners, bosses, and pass-through transitions where compression normally varies. Check that the seal height stays consistent around the connector opening and dowel points so no local high spot forces the lid to bridge over one section of the enclosure. |
| T5 | Refit the lid and tighten the fasteners in the intended cross pattern, watching the seal-compression line, lid-to-base gap, and pass-through corners so no one side closes early while another remains visibly high, then perform the specified splash or low-pressure leak confirmation on the enclosure boundary. The release check has to prove both uniform compression and a dry enclosure path at the original leak side rather than only a visually closed lid. |
| T6 | Release the controller area only after the work order includes the seal lot, joint location, flange condition, dowel alignment result, tightening sequence, pass-through check, original leak side, and final closure result for the BMS enclosure seal replacement. The accepted boundary is an enclosure that closes evenly, keeps the new gasket seated through every corner transition, and shows no renewed moisture path at the previously identified leak zone. |

evidence_records.raw_text:
| Time step | O&M sample text |
|---|---|
| T1 | Define the drive-motor coolant-hose leak on Helion DriveUnit-586 Plus by capturing whether it is active hot, active cold, pressure-dependent, or only visible after soak, and by noting the as-found condition of the motor-side hose routing, clamp position, and surrounding residue field before cleaning begins. |
| T2 | Isolate the traction system, stabilize the local cooling branch, and remove only the shields needed to expose the drive-motor coolant hose, hose clamp, tube neck, support bracket, and adjacent wiring without changing the hose twist from its as-found condition. |
| T3 | Clean only the loose surface residue and trace the wet edge from the hose neck outward, distinguishing seepage at the clamp line, coolant tracking along the hose braid, wetting at the tube bead, or migration from a higher branch above the drive unit. |
| T4 | Apply pressure to the local cooling branch and hold it long enough to see whether fresh wetting starts at the clamp gap, the hose-to-neck interface, the molded hose body, or the bracket-supported bend where the hose is forced off-axis. |
| T5 | Release the clamp only as far as required to inspect the band imprint, hose inner seat, and tube-bead contact area, checking for debris, inner-liner damage, corrosion at the bead, or a double imprint that would explain a repeat leak even with correct external clamp appearance. |
| T6 | Compare the suspect hose joint with the opposite or adjacent cooling branch, checking clamp clocking, insertion depth to the bead, hose twist, bracket alignment, and any polished witness mark showing that the hose was rubbing or being pulled in service. |
| T7 | Inspect the hose clamp, tube bead, hose inner seat, support bracket, and nearby sleeve for deformation, corrosion, clamp overtravel, or misposition that would separate a true leak joint from a routing-induced strain condition. |
| T8 | Relax the bracket side load if required and repeat the pressure check with the hose returned to its natural angle so the leak decision reflects the true joint condition and not an artificially loaded support geometry. |
| T9 | Record the exact leak boundary as hose clamp line, hose body, tube bead interface, or bracket-induced side load, and make sure the final note explains how the pressure test and the as-found residue pattern support that conclusion. |
| T10 | Restore only the protection needed for safe closure and leave the identified hose fault path visible and documented for the next repair action instead of hiding it under a broad coolant-hose leak label. |
